title: Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development
subtitle: Committed bilateral [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development

Committed bilateral [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

The stacked area chart tracks committed official development assistance from 1971 through 2024, with values adjusted to constant 2023 US dollars. The vertical axis measures funding in billions, ranging from zero to $200 billion, while the horizontal axis marks the timeline across five decades. Two distinct categories comprise the total volume: a lower pink band representing aid for emergencies and a dominant upper green section denoting aid for longer-term development. Throughout the entire period, the green area forms the vast majority of the total height, showing that long-term development projects consistently receive more funding than emergency relief. The total aid volume begins near $50 billion in 1971 and follows a generally upward trajectory, punctuated by a sharp spike around 1991 that pushes the total close to $100 billion before receding. From the early 2000s onward, the aggregate amount climbs more steeply, crossing the $120 billion mark around 2005 and reaching a peak just above $180 billion in the early 2020s. The pink band for emergency aid remains relatively thin and flat for the first thirty years, hovering near the bottom of the chart, but shows a visible increase in thickness starting around 2015 and expanding further toward 2024. Despite this growth in emergency funding, the green area for longer-term development maintains its position as the primary component of the total, driving the overall rise in global aid commitments over the observed timeframe.
